tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/erk/branch-naming.md
All erk-managed plan branches use the `plnd/` prefix. Plan issue numbers are **not** encoded in branch names

Use plan-ref.json as the sole source of truth." 12→last_audited: "2026-02-24 00:00 PT" 13→audit_result: clean 14→--- 15→ 16→# Branch Naming Conventions 17→ 18→All erk-managed plan branches use the `plnd/` prefix. Accepts `title`, `timestamp`, and optional `objective_id` keyword argument. 25→ 26→## Branch Format 27→ 28→**Without objective:** 29→ 30→```

tool_use.name: Edit
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/erk/issue-pr-linkage-storage.md

# Issue-PR Linkage Storage Model
This document describes how erk creates and stores the relationship between GitHub issues (plans) and pull requests.

# Plan-PR Linkage Storage Model
This document describes how erk creates and stores the relationship between plans and their implementation pull requests.

tool_use.name: Edit
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/erk/issue-pr-linkage-storage.md
- PRs to automatically close their source issue when merged
- PRs to automatically close their source plan when merged

tool_use.name: Edit
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/erk/issue-pr-linkage-storage.md
This file maps the current worktree to its source GitHub issue.
This file maps the current worktree to its source plan.

tool_use.name: Edit
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/erk/issue-pr-linkage-storage.md
Uses GraphQL to query `CrossReferencedEvent` timeline items on each plan issue:
Uses GraphQL to query `CrossReferencedEvent` timeline items on each plan:

tool_use.name: Edit
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/erk/issue-pr-linkage-storage.md
1. Fetches all issues with `erk-plan` label 2. For each issue, queries timeline for cross-references
1. Fetches all plans with `erk-plan` label 2. For each plan, queries timeline for cross-references

tool_use.name: Edit
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/configuration/issues-repo.md

# External Issues Repository Configuration
Configure erk to store plan issues in a separate repository from your working repository.

# External Plans Repository Configuration
Configure erk to store plans in a separate repository from your working repository.

tool_use.name: Edit
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/configuration/issues-repo.md
**Format:** `"owner/repo"` - the GitHub repository where plan issues will be created.
**Format:** `"owner/repo"` - the GitHub repository where plans will be created.

tool_use.name: Edit
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/configuration/issues-repo.md
- Plan issues are created in the target repository instead of the working repository
- Plans are created in the target repository instead of the working repository

tool_use.name: Edit
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/configuration/issues-repo.md
Erk uses these labels to organize plan issues:
Erk uses these labels to organize plans:

tool_use.name: Edit
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/configuration/issues-repo.md

**Centralized planning:** Store all plan issues in a dedicated repository while implementi...[truncated 140 chars]...eparation:** Allow broader access to plan issues while restricting code repository access.

**Centralized planning:** Store all plans in a dedicated repository while implementing in ...[truncated 122 chars]...cess separation:** Allow broader access to plans while restricting code repository access.

session.assistant: The file was read earlier in a batch call but seems to have been evicted. Let me re-read and edit:

tool_use.name: Read
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/configuration/issues-repo.md

tool_use.name: Read
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/integrations/issue-pr-closing-integration.md

tool_use.name: Read
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/reference/changelog-standards.md

tool_use.name: Read
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/sessions/discovery-fallback.md

session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→title: External Issues Repository Configuration 3→last_audited: "2026-02-17 09:00 PT" 4→audit_result: clean 5→read_when: 6→ - "configuring plans to be stored in a separate repository" 7→ - "setting up plans.repo in config.toml" 8→--- 9→ 10→# External Issues Repository Configuration 11→ 12→Configure erk to store plan issues in a separate repository from your working repository. 13→ 14→## Configuration 15→ 16→In `.erk/config.toml`: 17→ 18→```toml 19→[plans] 20→repo = "owner/repo" 21→``` 22→ 23→**Format:** `"owner/repo"` - the GitHub repository where plan issues will be created. 24→ 25→**Example:** 26→ 27→```toml 28→[plans] 29→repo = "myorg/engineering-plans" 30→```
... [54 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Issue-PR Closing Integration 3→read_when: 4→ - linking PRs to issues for auto-close 5→ - debugging why issues didn't close when PR merged 6→ - working on issue number discovery in the submit pipeline 7→ - understanding cross-repo plan issue references 8→tripwires: 9→ - action: "putting Closes keyword in PR title or commit message" 10→ warning: "GitHub only processes closing keywords in the PR body. Title and commit message references are ignored." 11→ - action: "using issue number from .erk/impl-context/plan-ref.json for a checkout footer" 12→ warning: "The checkout footer requires the PR number, not the issue number. These are different values — the issue is the plan, the PR is the implementation." 13→ - action: "resolving issue number from a single source without checking for mismatches" 14→ warning: "Both .erk/impl-context/plan-ref.json and branch name may contain issue numbers. If both exist, they must agree — otherwise the pipeline could silently close the wrong issue." 15→--- 16→ 17→# Issue-PR Closing Integration 18→ 19→GitHub automatically closes issues when a PR merges **only if** the closing keyword (`Closes #N`) appears in the **PR body**. Title and commit message placement has no effect. The PR must also merge to the repository's default branch. 20→ 21→This document covers the cross-cutting pattern of how erk ensures that closing references are correctly discovered, validated, and embedded across the submit pipeline, exec scripts, and footer generation system. 22→ 23→## Issue Number Discovery: Three Sources, One Truth 24→ 25→The submit pipeline resolves issue numbers from three sources with a strict priority hierarchy. The cross-cutting concern is that these sources live in different systems (filesystem, git, GitHub API) and must be validated against each other. 26→ 27→| Source | When used | Why it exists | 28→| --------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- | 29→| `.erk/impl-context/plan-ref.json` | Authoritative when present | Created by `erk br co --for-plan` or `erk pr submit` — explicit plan linkage | 30→| Branch name (`P{N}-slug`) | Fallback when no `.erk/impl-context/` | Supports manually-created worktrees from plan branches |
... [72 lines omitted] ...
35→**The mismatch guard is the key design decision.** When both `.erk/impl-context/plan-ref.json` and the branch name contain issue numbers, they must agree — a mismatch raises `ValueError` and halts the pipeline. This prevents the most dangerous failure mode: silently closing the wrong issue because stale metadata disagrees with the branch. See `validate_plan_linkage()` in `packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/impl_folder.py`. 74→## Debugging Auto-Close Failures 76→Check these conditions in order — the first failure found is almost always the cause: 89→The most common failure: the keyword is absent from the body because the PR was created outside erk's submit pipeline (e.g., via `gh pr create` directly), bypassing footer generation entirely.

1→--- 2→title: Changelog Standards and Format 3→read_when: 4→ - "writing CHANGELOG.md entries manually" 5→ - "understanding the changelog sync marker system" 6→ - "deciding how to format a changelog entry" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "adding a changelog entry without a commit hash reference" 9→ warning: "All unreleased entries must include 9-character short hashes in parentheses. Hashes are stripped at release time by /local:changelog-release." 10→ - action: "writing implementation-focused changelog entries" 11→ warning: "Entries must describe user-visible behavior, not internal implementation. Ask: 'Does an erk user see different behavior?'" 12→ - action: "modifying CHANGELOG.md directly instead of using /local:changelog-update" 13→ warning: "Always use /local:changelog-update to sync with commits. Manual edits bypass the categorization agent and marker system." 14→last_audited: "2026-02-16 00:00 PT" 15→audit_result: clean 16→--- 17→ 18→# Changelog Standards and Format 19→ 20→CHANGELOG.md follows [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/) with erk-specific conventions. This document explains the design decisions behind the format — for the authoritative entry format and workflow steps, see the command files linked below. 21→ 22→## System Architecture 23→ 24→The changelog system has three components that must stay coordinated: 25→ 26→| Component | Role | Authority over | 27→| -------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- | 28→| `/local:changelog-update` | Syncs unreleased section with new commits | Update workflow, entry format, marker management | 29→| `/local:changelog-release` | Finalizes unreleased into versioned section | Release format, hash stripping, version bumping | 30→| Commit categorizer agent | Classifies commits into categories | Category assignment, filtering rules, roll-up detection |
... [237 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Session Discovery and Fallback Patterns 3→read_when: 4→ - "implementing session analysis or learn workflows" 5→ - "handling missing or unavailable session files" 6→ - "choosing between session discovery commands" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "using get-session-metadata or get-session-for-issue exec commands" 9→ warning: "These commands do not exist. Use 'erk exec list-sessions' for general enumeration or 'erk exec get-learn-sessions' for plan-specific discovery." 10→ - action: "assuming a session ID from metadata corresponds to a file on disk" 11→ warning: "Claude Code manages session lifecycle; old sessions may be cleaned up. Always use LBYL discovery before reading." 12→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 13→audit_result: clean 14→--- 15→ 16→# Session Discovery and Fallback Patterns 17→ 18→## Core Principle 19→ 20→**Always discover what's available before assuming availability.** Session files are session-scoped — Claude Code may clean them up at any time, so a session ID from GitHub metadata may not correspond to a file on disk. Discovery commands exist specifically to bridge this gap. 21→ 22→## Two Discovery Paths 23→ 24→Erk has two distinct session discovery commands that serve different purposes. Choosing the wrong one wastes tokens or misses available sessions. 25→ 26→| Command | Purpose | Output | When to use | 27→| ------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | 28→| `erk exec list-sessions` | Enumerate all sessions for the current project | JSON with branch context and session metadata | General session browsing, selecting sessions interactively | 29→| `erk exec get-learn-sessions <issue>` | Find sessions associated with a specific plan issue | JSON with categorized session IDs and paths | Learn workflows, plan-specific analysis | 30→
... [83 lines omitted] ...
90→### Anti-pattern: Hard failure on missing session 92→Never `exit 1` or raise when a session file is unavailable. The entire fallback hierarchy exists because session availability is inherently unreliable. Log a warning and degrade to the next level.
session.assistant: Now let me make all the remaining edits for phases 6-9 in parallel:

tool_use.name: Edit
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/integrations/issue-pr-closing-integration.md

## Issue Number Discovery: Three Sources, One Truth
The submit pipeline resolves issue numbers from three sources with a strict priority hierarchy.

tool_use.name: Edit
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/integrations/issue-pr-closing-integration.md
**The mismatch guard is the key design decision.** When both `.erk/impl-context/plan-ref.json` and the branch name contain issue numbers, they must agree

tool_use.name: Edit
tool_use.param: /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/integrations/issue-pr-closing-integration.md
The footer needs both the issue number (for `Closes #N`) and the PR number (for the `erk pr checkout` command)

tool_use.name: Bash
tool_use.param: erk docs sync

session.tool_result: docs/learned/cli/workflow-commands.md:67:This is a cross-cutting concern — the launch command doesn't know about plans, but the metadata helper silently links workflow runs to their originating plan issues. The function uses multiple LBYL early returns (no matching branch pattern, no node ID available, no plan-header metadata block) to gracefully skip non-plan branches. docs/learned/reference/gastown-analysis.md:710:- Add severity field to plan issues docs/learned/reference/gastown-analysis.md:761:- Plan issues open too long docs/learned/planning/learn-plan-validation.md:18:Learn plans exist to extract insights from **implementation work** (erk-plan issues). A learn plan must never target another learn plan — this would create documentation cycles where plans endlessly analyze each other instead of analyzing real code changes. docs/learned/planning/cross-repo-plans.md:11:Store plan issues in a dedicated repository while implementing in another. docs/learned/planning/cross-repo-plans.md:15:- **Private plans**: Keep plan issues in a private repo while implementing in a public repo docs/learned/planning/cross-repo-plans.md:28:Where `owner/plans-repo` is the GitHub repository that will hold plan issues. docs/learned/planning/index.md:11:- **[branch-name-inference.md](branch-name-inference.md)** — debugging missing branch_name in plans, implementing PR lookup from plan issues, modifying branch creation or naming conventions docs/learned/planning/plan-backend-migration.md:18:When exec scripts need to read or update plan issues, use `PlanBackend` instead of direct `GitHubIssues` calls. PlanBackend provides typed abstractions with discriminated union error handling. docs/learned/planning/plan-schema.md:15:Plan issues use a two-part structure optimized for GitHub API performance: docs/learned/planning/branch-name-inference.md:14: - "implementing PR lookup from plan issues" docs/learned/objectives/objective-roadmap-check.md:42:| `erk-objective` label | Prevents running check on plan issues or random issues that happen to contain markdown tables | docs/learned/architecture/github-issue-autoclose.md:12:Erk previously used `Closes #N` in PR bodies to auto-close plan issues on merge. This approach was abandoned due to unreliable behavior. docs/learned/architecture/github-issue-autoclose.md:26:Plan issues are now closed directly via the GitHub API during `erk land`: docs/learned/architecture/plan-context-integration.md:16:The `PlanContextProvider` extracts plan content for branches linked to erk-plan issues, enabling more accurate PR descriptions that understand the "why" behind changes.

docs/learned/planning/no-changes-handling.md:30:4. **Posts issue comment**: Notifies the plan issue with a link to the PR docs/learned/planning/no-changes-handling.md:55:2. **If already done**: Close both the PR and the linked plan issue docs/learned/planning/cross-artifact-analysis.md:21:| Linked | PR explicitly references plan issue | PR body contains `Plan: #<issue>` reference | docs/learned/tui/data-contract.md:160:| `learn_plan_issue` | `int` | Yes | Plan issue number (for `completed_with_plan` status) | docs/learned/tui/data-contract.md:161:| `learn_plan_issue_closed` | `bool` | Yes | Whether the learn plan issue is closed | docs/learned/ci/learn-ci-environment-detection.md:55:**Why skip confirmations entirely?** The learn workflow was designed to be safe to run automatically — it creates a plan issue but doesn't merge code or modify production state. Auto-proceeding in CI reduces friction without introducing risk. docs/learned/planning/plan-header-privatization.md:14:The `plan_header.py` module contains low-level functions for reading/writing plan issue metadata blocks. These are being gradually privatized behind the `PlanBackend` abstraction. docs/learned/tui/plan-row-data.md:94:| `learn_plan_issue` | `int \| None` | Plan issue number (for completed_with_plan status) | Yes | docs/learned/tui/plan-row-data.md:95:| `learn_plan_issue_closed` | `bool \| None` | Whether the learn plan issue is closed | Yes | docs/learned/planning/workflow-markers.md:35:### Plan Issue Tracking docs/learned/planning/pr-discovery.md:19:Finding the PR associated with a plan issue is a cross-cutting concern that spans multiple commands (`get-pr-for-plan`, `trigger-async-learn`, `erk pr co`, `erk pr close`). Two fundamentally different strategies exist, chosen based on what metadata is available. docs/learned/planning/consolidation-labels.md:34:The label application happens in Step 7 of `/erk:replan`, after the new plan issue is created via `/erk:plan-save`. docs/learned/planning/session-preprocessing.md:77:The planning session ID is captured at plan creation time by the `session_id_injector_hook`, which writes the current session ID to `.erk/scratch/current-session-id`. This is then embedded in the plan issue's metadata when the issue is created. docs/learned/planning/planned-pr-context-local-preprocessing.md:53:**Graceful degradation**: If no PR exists for the plan issue, comment fetching is skipped entirely. Missing PR comments don't block the learn workflow — sessions alone contain sufficient material for insight extraction. docs/learned/planning/learn-plan-validation.md:18:Learn plans exist to extract insights from **implementation work** (erk-plan issues). A learn plan must never target another learn plan — this would create documentation cycles where plans endlessly analyze each other instead of analyzing real code changes. docs/learned/planning/plan-schema.md:15:Plan issues use a two-part structure optimized for GitHub API performance: docs/learned/configuration/config-layers.md:51:- `[plans]` - Plan issue repository settings docs/learned/planning/learn-pipeline-workflow.md:27:The learn pipeline has 7 stages that transform raw session JSONL into a documentation plan issue. The key architectural decision is **where processing happens**: stages 1-5 run locally (fast, free), while stages 6-7 run in GitHub Actions (background, billed). docs/learned/planning/learn-pipeline-workflow.md:60:Finds all session logs related to a plan issue by querying GitHub issue metadata for tracked session IDs, then resolving those IDs to local file paths. Three session source types exist: docs/learned/planning/learn-pipeline-workflow.md:118:Human reviews the learn plan issue, optionally edits it, and submits for implementation through the standard `plan-implement` workflow. docs/learned/planning/branch-name-inference.md:14: - "implementing PR lookup from plan issues" docs/learned/planning/branch-name-inference.md:32:When code needs to look up a PR from a plan issue, `branch_name` may be missing from metadata. The system uses two layers: docs/learned/pr-operations/plan-embedding-in-pr.md:18:When a PR originates from an erk-plan issue, reviewers need the original plan for context — it explains the _intent_ behind the code, not just the diff. Embedding the plan directly in the PR body means reviewers don't have to chase cross-references to a separate GitHub issue. A collapsible `<details>` section keeps this context available without overwhelming the PR description. docs/learned/pr-operations/plan-embedding-in-pr.md:25:2. **GitHub API** → fetches the plan issue body, then the plan comment containing the actual plan markdown docs/learned/planning/tripwires.md:95:**closing a plan issue without verifying all items were addressed** → Read [Complete File Inventory Protocol](complete-inventory-protocol.md) first. Compare the file inventory against the plan's items before closing. Silent omissions are the most common failure mode. docs/learned/pr-operations/pr-validation-rules.md:9: warning: "Checkout footers require the PR number (from create_pr return value), NOT the plan issue number. See pr-validation-rules.md." docs/learned/pr-operations/pr-submit-phases.md:60:The `PlanContextProvider` checks for a linked erk-plan issue: docs/learned/pr-operations/checkout-footer-syntax.md:41:| `.erk/impl-context/plan-ref.json` | Never | Plan issue number != PR number; validation will fail | docs/learned/pr-operations/tripwires.md:41:**using issue number from .erk/impl-context/plan-ref.json in a checkout footer** → Read [PR Validation Rules](pr-validation-rules.md) first. Checkout footers require the PR number (from create_pr return value), NOT the plan issue number. See pr-validation-rules.md. docs/learned/objectives/objective-lifecycle.md:437:3. **Objective**: extracted from plan issue metadata (`plan-header.objective_issue` field)
... [62 lines omitted] ...
docs/learned/planning/plan-backend-migration.md:18:When exec scripts need to read or update plan issues, use `PlanBackend` instead of direct `GitHubIssues` calls. PlanBackend provides typed abstractions with discriminated union error handling. docs/learned/erk/pr-commands.md:10: warning: "Checkout footer requires the PR number (from gh pr create output), NOT the plan issue number from .erk/impl-context/plan-ref.json." docs/learned/workflows/one-shot-workflow.md:10: warning: "Branch format is `oneshot-{slug}-{MM-DD-HHMM}` (no plan issue) or `P{N}-{slug}-{MM-DD-HHMM}` (when plan_issue_number is provided). The workflow and CLI both depend on these prefixes for identification."
session.assistant:
There are many remaining "plan issue" references outside the files covered by the plan (Nodes 6.5 + 6.6). The plan explicitly states:
> **Key principle**: Only change "issue" when it refers to a *plan*. Objective issues are still GitHub Issues. GitHub's `Closes #N` feature references are legitimate. Function/variable names in code examples stay as-is (those are Phase 7 work).
The plan covers specific files (glossary.md, conventions.md, configuration/issues-repo.md, integrations/issue-pr-closing-integration.md, reference/changelog-standards.md, erk/branch-naming.md, erk/issue-pr-linkage-storage.md, sessions/discovery-fallback.md, and 7 planning frontmatter files). The remaining references in other files (like tui/, cli/, architecture/, etc.) are outside the scope of Nodes 6.5 + 6.6.
However, I see that `planning/branch-name-inference.md` line 14 still has "plan issues" in a `read_when` that wasn't in the plan. Let me also check if `planning/cross-repo-plans.md` body has references not covered. The plan only covered cross-repo-plans.md frontmatter, not its body:
